decision_source: Whether to call the Anthropic API directly from client-side JavaScript or route all API calls through a server-side proxy
stem: A startup's frontend developer ships a web chat application that calls the Anthropic API directly from the browser, embedding the API key in the JavaScript bundle to avoid standing up a backend. Within 48 hours, the team receives an alert about a billing spike 40x above their normal usage. A post-incident review confirms the key was extracted from the bundle by a third party. What architectural decision caused this failure?

options.A: The team failed to set a hard spending cap in the Anthropic console before launch, which would have blocked unauthorized usage
options.B: The API key was exposed in the client-side bundle, making it publicly accessible to anyone who inspects the browser's network traffic or source
options.C: The team should have used a restricted API key scoped to their application's domain to prevent cross-origin usage
options.D: The frontend made requests without HTTPS, allowing the key to be intercepted in transit

decision_source: Whether to call the Anthropic API directly from client-side JavaScript or route all API calls through a server-side proxy
stem: Your team is building a customer-facing web app that uses Claude to summarize support tickets. A frontend engineer proposes calling the Anthropic API directly from the browser using a JavaScript `fetch()` request, embedding the API key in the client-side bundle to avoid the latency of an extra server hop. The backend engineer argues all Claude calls should be routed through a server-side proxy, similar to how Starter Cell 1 in the lab keeps the key in `os.environ` rather than in code the client ever touches. Which architectural concern most directly justifies the backend engineer's position?

options.A: Browser-based API calls cannot set the `system` parameter, so the summarization prompt would be silently ignored by Claude
options.B: Embedding the API key in a client-side bundle exposes it to any user who inspects the network traffic or JavaScript source, enabling unrestricted use of the team's account
options.C: Routing through a server-side proxy allows the backend to cache Claude responses in the browser's local storage, reducing per-request token costs
options.D: Direct browser calls to the Anthropic API violate CORS policy unconditionally, causing every request to fail regardless of how the key is stored

decision_source: Whether to specify explicit output format instructions in a prompt or rely on Claude's default formatting behavior
stem: A team ships a customer support summarization tool that feeds Claude-generated briefings into an executive dashboard. The dashboard renders a fixed 200-word card with no markdown support. During QA, the team notices that short tickets produce two-sentence responses while complex tickets produce 600-word markdown reports with headers and bullet points — some responses render correctly, others overflow their containers or display raw asterisks. The team already has a post-processing normalization layer that strips markdown and truncates to 200 words, but the layer is still producing failures because truncation mid-sentence creates incoherent summaries that executives are complaining about. A junior engineer argues the inconsistency will resolve itself once real tickets are submitted, since Claude will 'learn' the expected style from usage patterns. What is the most architecturally sound resolution to the root cause of these failures?

options.A: Move the format instructions to the user-turn prompt so they can be tuned per ticket type without modifying the system prompt.
options.B: Add explicit format constraints to the system prompt specifying maximum word count, plain-text only, required sections, and the executive audience — applied uniformly to every request.
options.C: Lower the model temperature so that output length and structure become more predictable across varying ticket lengths.
options.D: Replace the post-processing truncation logic with a sentence-boundary detection algorithm so summaries always end at a complete sentence.

decision_source: Whether to specify explicit output format instructions in a prompt or rely on Claude's default formatting behavior
stem: In the lab, you called `client.messages.create()` with a system prompt and a user message containing a raw support ticket. You observed that responses varied significantly in length and structure across different ticket inputs — some returned plain sentences, others returned markdown bullet lists. You are now extending this into a production summarization pipeline where output is rendered inside a fixed-width plain-text email template. Which change to your `client.messages.create()` call most directly resolves the formatting variance for this production requirement?

options.A: Add `stream=True` to the `client.messages.create()` call so the rendering layer can stop consuming tokens once the 200-word limit is reached.
options.B: Set `max_tokens` to a low value in `client.messages.create()` so responses are physically capped before they can exceed the email template's length limit.
options.C: Update the `system` parameter in `client.messages.create()` to explicitly specify plain-text format, maximum word count, required sections, and target audience — ensuring every response conforms to the email template regardless of ticket content.
options.D: Pass the previous response back in the `messages` array as an assistant turn so Claude can reference the format it used last time and replicate it.

decision_source: Whether to include explicit persona, audience, and negative constraints in a prompt or rely on a minimal instruction set for a content moderation classifier
stem: A platform's content moderation pipeline sends user-generated posts to Claude with the prompt 'Classify this post as safe, borderline, or violating.' During QA, engineers observe that roughly 8% of responses include explanatory sentences like 'This post appears borderline — it mentions sensitive topics but does not explicitly violate guidelines' instead of a single token. The downstream parser throws an exception on any response that is not exactly one of the three enumerated values, silently dropping the moderation decision. What is the most effective prompt engineering change to eliminate this failure mode?

options.A: Add a post-processing regex extractor that scans Claude's free-text response and pulls the first matching taxonomy term, removing the need to change the prompt.
options.B: Extend the prompt with explicit negative constraints ('respond only with one of: safe, borderline, violating — no explanations, no questions, no other text') and specify that the output is consumed by an automated pipeline, not a human reader.
options.C: Lower the model's temperature to reduce output variance, since a more deterministic sampling process will naturally suppress verbose responses.
options.D: Replace Claude with a fine-tuned classification model trained on the three-label taxonomy, because instruction-following models are architecturally unsuited to single-token output tasks.

decision_source: Whether to include explicit persona, audience, and negative constraints in a prompt or rely on a minimal instruction set for a content moderation classifier
stem: In the lab, you initialized the Anthropic client and constructed API calls with an explicit system prompt passed as the 'system' parameter. Your team is now building a content moderation classifier and debates whether the system prompt alone — set once at client initialization — is sufficient to prevent Claude from returning explanatory text alongside the classification label. A colleague argues that because the system prompt persists across every call in the session, repeating format constraints in each user message is redundant overhead. What is the most accurate assessment of this approach for a zero-tolerance classification pipeline?

options.A: The colleague is correct: a well-written system prompt is architecturally equivalent to per-message constraints, and repeating instructions increases token cost with no reliability benefit.
options.B: The system prompt should define the classifier role and enumerated taxonomy, but explicit negative constraints — prohibiting explanations and specifying automated-pipeline audience — must also appear in the system prompt to reliably suppress verbose output; relying on implicit inference from role alone is insufficient.
options.C: Format constraints should be omitted from the system prompt entirely and enforced exclusively through the structured output API parameter, because mixing prompt-based and API-level controls creates conflicting instructions.
options.D: Passing a longer system prompt increases the likelihood that Claude will hallucinate category labels outside the defined taxonomy, so the system prompt should remain as short as possible.

decision_source: How to manage conversation history length in a multi-turn chat application to prevent context window exhaustion without losing critical conversational continuity
stem: A customer support platform deploys a Claude-powered bot that handles complex billing disputes. Post-launch telemetry shows that 23% of sessions exceeding 40 turns result in the bot asking users to re-explain their original complaint — a problem not observed in QA testing, which capped sessions at 15 turns. Engineering determines the bot is hitting token limits and the oldest messages are being dropped from the conversation array. The product team demands a fix that preserves resolution quality without significantly increasing per-session cost. Which architectural approach best addresses the root cause while satisfying both constraints?

options.A: Switch all sessions to the largest available context window model, eliminating token limit errors regardless of conversation length
options.B: Implement progressive summarization: compress older conversation segments into a running summary injected at the top of context, retaining full message fidelity only for recent turns
options.C: Increase the truncation threshold so the system retains the last 30 turns instead of 15, giving the model more recent context before messages are dropped
options.D: Add a retrieval step that stores all messages in a vector database and fetches the top-k most semantically similar past turns at each new message

decision_source: How to manage conversation history length in a multi-turn chat application to prevent context window exhaustion without losing critical conversational continuity
stem: In the multi-turn conversation exercise you built, your conversation_history list is passed directly into the messages parameter on each API call. During load testing you simulate a 60-turn support session and observe an API error indicating the request exceeds the model's context window. You add a guard that slices conversation_history to the last 20 entries before each call. In a follow-up test, the bot correctly handles turn 55 but asks the user at turn 56 to confirm their account number — information the user provided at turn 3, which is now outside the retained window. What is the most architecturally sound fix that preserves the integrity of the multi-turn loop you constructed?

options.A: Before slicing conversation_history, make a separate API call to Claude asking it to summarize the entries about to be removed, then prepend that summary as a system-level user message at index 0 of the retained history
options.B: Replace the slice with a token-counting check: count tokens in conversation_history before each call and only truncate if the count exceeds 80% of the model's limit, preserving more turns on average
options.C: Store the full conversation_history in a database and pass only the last 20 turns to the API; rely on the model to infer missing context from the recent turns it does receive
options.D: Increase max_tokens in your API call parameters to give the model more room to generate longer responses, which will reduce the frequency of context overflow errors
